raw_text.response:
Let  

\[
\mathcal R=\{A\in\mathbb R^{3\times 3}\;:\;A\text{ is in reduced row‑echelon form}\}
\]

and consider \(\mathcal R\) as a subset of the vector space  
\(M_{3\times 3}(\mathbb R)\cong\mathbb R^{9}\).

To be a subspace \(\mathcal R\) must satisfy the three axioms (closure under
addition, closure under scalar multiplication and contain the zero vector)
that are part of the vector‑space axioms (see the list in the definition of a
vector space, Document 3).

*Zero vector.*  
The zero matrix is in reduced row‑echelon form, so \(\mathbf 0\in\mathcal R\).

*Scalar multiplication.*  
Take  

\[
A=\begin{pmatrix}
1&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0
\end{pmatrix}\in\mathcal R .
\]

For the scalar \(\lambda =2\),

\[
\lambda A=
\begin{pmatrix}
2&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0
\end{pmatrix}
\]

has a leading entry equal to \(2\) instead of \(1\); therefore \(\lambda A\)
is **not** in reduced row‑echelon form. Hence \(\mathcal R\) is not closed
under scalar multiplication (except for \(\lambda =0,1\)).

*Addition.*  
Even if we restrict to scalars that preserve the “leading‑1’’ condition,
addition can destroy the RREF property.  For instance

\[
A=\begin{pmatrix}
1&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0
\end{pmatrix},\qquad
B=\begin{pmatrix}
1&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0
\end{pmatrix}
\]

are both in \(\mathcal R\), but  

\[
A+B=\begin{pmatrix}
2&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0\\[2pt]
0&0&0
\end{pmatrix}
\]

is not in reduced row‑echelon form for the same reason as above.  Thus
\(\mathcal R\) is not closed under addition either.

Since \(\mathcal R\) fails the closure properties required by the vector‑space
axioms, it cannot be a linear subspace of \(\mathbb R^{3\times 3}\).

\[
\boxed{\text{The set of all }3\times3\text{ matrices in reduced row‑echelon form is \emph{not} a subspace of }\mathbb R^{9}.}
\]
